narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 38, which edges out the average of 36.70, you've put together a competitive profile in many ways, including your middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 68.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in their program. The model's decision hinged most on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school, particularly your Year 3 performance, which landed you in the bottom decile (1 out of 10) compared to the average student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile. This low Year 3 decile had the strongest pull toward predicting bar failure, as it signaled to the model a potential struggle with sustained high-level coursework that often foreshadows bar challenges. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom (1.00 versus the average 5.71), reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor, suggesting early academic hurdles that the model associates with higher failure risk. On a brighter note, your LSAT score pushed back against failure the most among the key influences, helping lower the predicted risk because it's above average and indicates strong test-taking ability relevant to the bar. Compared to typical students who pass, your early deciles are notably lower, pulling you into a riskier group despite your later strengths in GPA and LSAT. While factors like your full-time status align with the vast majority (over 90%) of successful students, they didn't outweigh the early academic flags here. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as improving early-grade performance has helped many similar applicants like you get approved later. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, given your solid LSAT foundation, and reapply after demonstrating stronger academic consistency—schools value growth stories from determined candidates. With your above-average GPA and LSAT, you're already ahead in key areas; focusing on those early deciles could flip the model's prediction in your favor next time.
